solution:
In fact, we will show that such a function $g$ exists with the
property that $(a,b,c) \in S$ if and only if $g(d) < g(e) < g(f)$ for
some cyclic permutation $(d,e,f)$ of $(a,b,c)$. We proceed by
induction on the number of elements in $A$. If $A =
\{a,b,c\}$ and $(a,b,c) \in S$, then choose $g$ with $g(a) < g(b) <
g(c)$, otherwise choose $g$ with $g(a) > g(b) > g(c)$.

Now let $z$ be an element of $A$ and $B = A - \{z\}$.
Let $a_{1}, \dots, a_{n}$ be the elements of $B$ labeled such that
$g(a_{1}) < g(a_{2}) < \cdots < g(a_{n})$. We claim that there exists
a unique $i \in \{1, \dots, n\}$ such that $(a_{i}, z, a_{i+1})
\in S$, where hereafter $a_{n+k} = a_{k}$.

We show existence first. Suppose no such $i$ exists; then for all
$i,k \in \{1, \dots, n\}$, we have $(a_{i+k}, z, a_{i}) \notin S$.
This holds by property 1 for $k=1$ and by induction on $k$ in
general, noting that
\begin{align*}
(a_{i+k+1}, z, a_{i+k}), &(a_{i+k}, z, a_{i})  \in S \\
&\Rightarrow (a_{i+k}, a_{i+k+1}, z), (z, a_{i}, a_{i+k}) \in S \\
&\Rightarrow (a_{i+k+1},z,a_{i}) \in S.
\end{align*}
Applying this when $k=n$, we get $(a_{i-1}, z, a_{i}) \in S$,
contradicting the fact that $(a_{i}, z, a_{i-1}) \in S$. Hence
existence follows.

Now we show uniqueness. Suppose $(a_{i}, z, a_{i+1}) \in S$; then for
any $j \neq i-1, i, i+1$, we have $(a_{i}, a_{i+1}, a_{j}), (a_{j},
a_{j+1}, a_{i}) \in S$ by the
assumption on $G$. Therefore
\begin{align*}
(a_{i}, z, a_{i+1}), (a_{i+1}, a_{j}, a_{i}) \in S
&\Rightarrow (a_{j}, a_{i}, z) \in S \\
(a_{i}, z, a_{j}), (a_{j}, a_{j+1}, a_{i}) \in S
&\Rightarrow (z, a_{j}, a_{j+1}),
\end{align*}
so $(a_{j}, z, a_{j+1}) \notin S$. The case $j =i+1$ is ruled out by
\[
(a_{i}, z, a_{i+1}), (a_{i+1}, a_{i+2}, a_{i}) \in S \Rightarrow (z,
a_{i+1}, a_{i+2}) \in S
\]
and the case $j=i-1$ is similar.

Finally, we put $g(z)$ in $(g(a_{n}), + \infty)$ if $i = n$, and
$(g(a_{i}), g(a_{i+1}))$ otherwise; an analysis similar to that above
shows that $g$ has the desired property.

descriptive_long.solution:
In fact, we will show that such a function $ordering$ exists with the property that $(elementa,elementb,elementc) \in tripleset$ if and only if $ordering(elementd) < ordering(elemente) < ordering(elementf)$ for some cyclic permutation $(elementd,elemente,elementf)$ of $(elementa,elementb,elementc)$. We proceed by induction on the number of elements in $bigseta$. If $bigseta = \{elementa,elementb,elementc\}$ and $(elementa,elementb,elementc) \in tripleset$, then choose $ordering$ with $ordering(elementa) < ordering(elementb) < ordering(elementc)$; otherwise choose $ordering$ with $ordering(elementa) > ordering(elementb) > ordering(elementc)$.

Now let $elementz$ be an element of $bigseta$ and set $subsetb = bigseta - \{elementz\}$. Let $elementone, \dots, elementn$ be the elements of $subsetb$ labeled so that
$ordering(elementone) < ordering(elementtwo) < \cdots < ordering(elementn)$. We claim that there exists a unique $indexi \in \{1, \dots, sizen\}$ such that $(elementa_{indexi}, elementz, elementa_{indexi+1}) \in tripleset$, where hereafter we agree that $elementa_{sizen+indexk} = elementa_{indexk}$.

Existence.  Suppose no such $indexi$ exists; then for all $indexi,indexk \in \{1, \dots, sizen\}$ we have $(elementa_{indexi+indexk}, elementz, elementa_{indexi}) \notin tripleset$.  This holds for $indexk=1$ by property~1 and for general $indexk$ by induction, noting that
\begin{align*}
(elementa_{indexi+indexk+1}, elementz, elementa_{indexi+indexk}), &(elementa_{indexi+indexk}, elementz, elementa_{indexi}) \in tripleset\\
&\Rightarrow (elementa_{indexi+indexk}, elementa_{indexi+indexk+1}, elementz), (elementz, elementa_{indexi}, elementa_{indexi+indexk}) \in tripleset\\
&\Rightarrow (elementa_{indexi+indexk+1}, elementz, elementa_{indexi}) \in tripleset.
\end{align*}
Taking $indexk = sizen$ yields $(elementa_{indexi-1}, elementz, elementa_{indexi}) \in tripleset$, contradicting $(elementa_{indexi}, elementz, elementa_{indexi-1}) \in tripleset$. Hence existence is proved.

Uniqueness.  Suppose $(elementa_{indexi}, elementz, elementa_{indexi+1}) \in tripleset$. For any $indexj \neq indexi-1, indexi, indexi+1$ we have $(elementa_{indexi}, elementa_{indexi+1}, elementa_{indexj}), (elementa_{indexj}, elementa_{indexj+1}, elementa_{indexi}) \in tripleset$ by the assumption on $assumgraph$. Therefore
\begin{align*}
(elementa_{indexi}, elementz, elementa_{indexi+1}), (elementa_{indexi+1}, elementa_{indexj}, elementa_{indexi}) &\in tripleset \;\Rightarrow\; (elementa_{indexj}, elementa_{indexi}, elementz) \in tripleset,\\
(elementa_{indexi}, elementz, elementa_{indexj}), (elementa_{indexj}, elementa_{indexj+1}, elementa_{indexi}) &\in tripleset \;\Rightarrow\; (elementz, elementa_{indexj}, elementa_{indexj+1}) \in tripleset,
\end{align*}
so $(elementa_{indexj}, elementz, elementa_{indexj+1}) \notin tripleset$.  The case $indexj = indexi+1$ is ruled out by
\[
(elementa_{indexi}, elementz, elementa_{indexi+1}), (elementa_{indexi+1}, elementa_{indexi+2}, elementa_{indexi}) \in tripleset \Rightarrow (elementz, elementa_{indexi+1}, elementa_{indexi+2}) \in tripleset,
\]
and the case $indexj = indexi-1$ is analogous. Hence the required $indexi$ is unique.

Finally, set $ordering(elementz)$ in $(ordering(elementn), +\infty)$ if $indexi = sizen$, and in $(ordering(elementa_{indexi}),\, ordering(elementa_{indexi+1}))$ otherwise.  An argument parallel to the one above shows that the resulting $ordering$ has the desired property.

descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
In fact, we will show that such a function $lanternmap$ exists with the
property that $(whaleroad,sunlitpath,moondapple) \in lanternset$ if and only if $lanternmap(breezehill) < lanternmap(stoneglade) < lanternmap(riverbloom)$ for
some cyclic permutation $(breezehill,stoneglade,riverbloom)$ of $(whaleroad,sunlitpath,moondapple)$. We proceed by
induction on the number of elements in $willowbank$. If $willowbank =
\{whaleroad,sunlitpath,moondapple\}$ and $(whaleroad,sunlitpath,moondapple) \in lanternset$, then choose $lanternmap$ with $lanternmap(whaleroad) < lanternmap(sunlitpath) <
lanternmap(moondapple)$, otherwise choose $lanternmap$ with $lanternmap(whaleroad) > lanternmap(sunlitpath) > lanternmap(moondapple)$.

Now let $embercloak$ be an element of $willowbank$ and $cedargrove = willowbank - \{embercloak\}$.
Let $whaleroad_{1}, \dots, whaleroad_{dovetailr}$ be the elements of $cedargrove$ labeled such that
$lanternmap(whaleroad_{1}) < lanternmap(whaleroad_{2}) < \cdots < lanternmap(whaleroad_{dovetailr})$. We claim that there exists
a unique $foxglovep \in \{1, \dots, dovetailr\}$ such that $(whaleroad_{foxglovep}, embercloak, whaleroad_{foxglovep+1})
\in lanternset$, where hereafter $whaleroad_{dovetailr+velvetdawn} = whaleroad_{velvetdawn}$.

We show existence first. Suppose no such $foxglovep$ exists; then for all
$foxglovep,velvetdawn \in \{1, \dots, dovetailr\}$, we have $(whaleroad_{foxglovep+velvetdawn}, embercloak, whaleroad_{foxglovep}) \notin lanternset$.
This holds by property 1 for $velvetdawn=1$ and by induction on $velvetdawn$ in
general, noting that
\begin{align*}
(whaleroad_{foxglovep+velvetdawn+1}, embercloak, whaleroad_{foxglovep+velvetdawn}), &(whaleroad_{foxglovep+velvetdawn}, embercloak, whaleroad_{foxglovep})  \in lanternset \\
&\Rightarrow (whaleroad_{foxglovep+velvetdawn}, whaleroad_{foxglovep+velvetdawn+1}, embercloak), (embercloak, whaleroad_{foxglovep}, whaleroad_{foxglovep+velvetdawn}) \in lanternset \\
&\Rightarrow (whaleroad_{foxglovep+velvetdawn+1},embercloak,whaleroad_{foxglovep}) \in lanternset.
\end{align*}
Applying this when $velvetdawn=dovetailr$, we get $(whaleroad_{foxglovep-1}, embercloak, whaleroad_{foxglovep}) \in lanternset$,
contradicting the fact that $(whaleroad_{foxglovep}, embercloak, whaleroad_{foxglovep-1}) \in lanternset$. Hence
existence follows.

Now we show uniqueness. Suppose $(whaleroad_{foxglovep}, embercloak, whaleroad_{foxglovep+1}) \in lanternset$; then for
any $crimsonark \neq foxglovep-1, foxglovep, foxglovep+1$, we have $(whaleroad_{foxglovep}, whaleroad_{foxglovep+1}, whaleroad_{crimsonark}), (whaleroad_{crimsonark},
whaleroad_{crimsonark+1}, whaleroad_{foxglovep}) \in lanternset$ by the
assumption on $frosthaven$. Therefore
\begin{align*}
(whaleroad_{foxglovep}, embercloak, whaleroad_{foxglovep+1}), (whaleroad_{foxglovep+1}, whaleroad_{crimsonark}, whaleroad_{foxglovep}) \in lanternset
&\Rightarrow (whaleroad_{crimsonark}, whaleroad_{foxglovep}, embercloak) \in lanternset \\
(whaleroad_{foxglovep}, embercloak, whaleroad_{crimsonark}), (whaleroad_{crimsonark}, whaleroad_{crimsonark+1}, whaleroad_{foxglovep}) \in lanternset
&\Rightarrow (embercloak, whaleroad_{crimsonark}, whaleroad_{crimsonark+1}),
\end{align*}
so $(whaleroad_{crimsonark}, embercloak, whaleroad_{crimsonark+1}) \notin lanternset$. The case $crimsonark =foxglovep+1$ is ruled out by
\[
(whaleroad_{foxglovep}, embercloak, whaleroad_{foxglovep+1}), (whaleroad_{foxglovep+1}, whaleroad_{foxglovep+2}, whaleroad_{foxglovep}) \in lanternset \Rightarrow (embercloak,
whaleroad_{foxglovep+1}, whaleroad_{foxglovep+2}) \in lanternset
\]
and the case $crimsonark=foxglovep-1$ is similar.

Finally, we put $lanternmap(embercloak)$ in $(lanternmap(whaleroad_{dovetailr}), + \infty)$ if $foxglovep = dovetailr$, and
$(lanternmap(whaleroad_{foxglovep}), lanternmap(whaleroad_{foxglovep+1}))$ otherwise; an analysis similar to that above
shows that $lanternmap$ has the desired property.

descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
In fact, we will show that such a function $disorderfn$ exists with the
property that $(omegaelem,alphachar,xenovalue) \in emptysup$ if and only if $disorderfn(terminalid) < disorderfn(westfield) < disorderfn(nullvalue)$ for
some cyclic permutation $(terminalid,westfield,nullvalue)$ of $(omegaelem,alphachar,xenovalue)$. We proceed by
induction on the number of elements in $infinitepool$. If $infinitepool =
\{omegaelem,alphachar,xenovalue\}$ and $(omegaelem,alphachar,xenovalue) \in emptysup$, then choose $disorderfn$ with $disorderfn(omegaelem) < disorderfn(alphachar) <
disorderfn(xenovalue)$, otherwise choose $disorderfn$ with $disorderfn(omegaelem) > disorderfn(alphachar) > disorderfn(xenovalue)$.

Now let $firstentry$ be an element of $infinitepool$ and $wholesetb = infinitepool - \{firstentry\}$.
Let $omegaelem_{1}, \dots, omegaelem_{zerobase}$ be the elements of $wholesetb$ labeled such that
$disorderfn(omegaelem_{1}) < disorderfn(omegaelem_{2}) < \cdots < disorderfn(omegaelem_{zerobase})$. We claim that there exists
a unique $outsider \in \{1, \dots, zerobase\}$ such that $(omegaelem_{outsider}, firstentry, omegaelem_{outsider+1})
\in emptysup$, where hereafter $omegaelem_{zerobase+centered} = omegaelem_{centered}$.

We show existence first. Suppose no such $outsider$ exists; then for all
$outsider,centered \in \{1, \dots, zerobase\}$, we have $(omegaelem_{outsider+centered}, firstentry, omegaelem_{outsider}) \notin emptysup$.
This holds by property 1 for $centered=1$ and by induction on $centered$ in
general, noting that
\begin{align*}
(omegaelem_{outsider+centered+1}, firstentry, omegaelem_{outsider+centered}), &(omegaelem_{outsider+centered}, firstentry, omegaelem_{outsider})  \in emptysup \\
&\Rightarrow (omegaelem_{outsider+centered}, omegaelem_{outsider+centered+1}, firstentry), (firstentry, omegaelem_{outsider}, omegaelem_{outsider+centered}) \in emptysup \\
&\Rightarrow (omegaelem_{outsider+centered+1},firstentry,omegaelem_{outsider}) \in emptysup.
\end{align*}
Applying this when $centered=zerobase$, we get $(omegaelem_{outsider-1}, firstentry, omegaelem_{outsider}) \in emptysup$,
contradicting the fact that $(omegaelem_{outsider}, firstentry, omegaelem_{outsider-1}) \in emptysup$. Hence
existence follows.

Now we show uniqueness. Suppose $(omegaelem_{outsider}, firstentry, omegaelem_{outsider+1}) \in emptysup$; then for
any $insidera \neq outsider-1, outsider, outsider+1$, we have $(omegaelem_{outsider}, omegaelem_{outsider+1}, omegaelem_{insidera}), (omegaelem_{insidera},
omegaelem_{insidera+1}, omegaelem_{outsider}) \in emptysup$ by the
assumption on $phantomset$. Therefore
\begin{align*}
(omegaelem_{outsider}, firstentry, omegaelem_{outsider+1}), (omegaelem_{outsider+1}, omegaelem_{insidera}, omegaelem_{outsider}) \in emptysup
&\Rightarrow (omegaelem_{insidera}, omegaelem_{outsider}, firstentry) \in emptysup \\
(omegaelem_{outsider}, firstentry, omegaelem_{insidera}), (omegaelem_{insidera}, omegaelem_{insidera+1}, omegaelem_{outsider}) \in emptysup
&\Rightarrow (firstentry, omegaelem_{insidera}, omegaelem_{insidera+1}),
\end{align*}
so $(omegaelem_{insidera}, firstentry, omegaelem_{insidera+1}) \notin emptysup$. The case $insidera =outsider+1$ is ruled out by
\[
(omegaelem_{outsider}, firstentry, omegaelem_{outsider+1}), (omegaelem_{outsider+1}, omegaelem_{outsider+2}, omegaelem_{outsider}) \in emptysup \Rightarrow (firstentry,
omegaelem_{outsider+1}, omegaelem_{outsider+2}) \in emptysup
\]
and the case $insidera=outsider-1$ is similar.

Finally, we put $disorderfn(firstentry)$ in $(disorderfn(omegaelem_{zerobase}), + \infty)$ if $outsider = zerobase$, and
$(disorderfn(omegaelem_{outsider}), disorderfn(omegaelem_{outsider+1}))$ otherwise; an analysis similar to that above
shows that $disorderfn$ has the desired property.

garbled_string.solution:
In fact, we will show that such a function $xmtrawob$ exists with the property that $(qzxwvtnp,hjgrksla,ptvfqion) \in lqmgidest$ if and only if $xmtrawob(lmsrkdbe) < xmtrawob(vfwyrzna) < xmtrawob(kgzlqmtp)$ for some cyclic permutation $(lmsrkdbe,vfwyrzna,kgzlqmtp)$ of $(qzxwvtnp,hjgrksla,ptvfqion)$. We proceed by induction on the number of elements in $qfztmxsa$. If $qfztmxsa = \{qzxwvtnp,hjgrksla,ptvfqion\}$ and $(qzxwvtnp,hjgrksla,ptvfqion) \in lqmgidest$, then choose $xmtrawob$ with $xmtrawob(qzxwvtnp) < xmtrawob(hjgrksla) < xmtrawob(ptvfqion)$, otherwise choose $xmtrawob$ with $xmtrawob(qzxwvtnp) > xmtrawob(hjgrksla) > xmtrawob(ptvfqion)$.

Now let $tnjgwepo$ be an element of $qfztmxsa$ and $rnszvpqa = qfztmxsa - \{tnjgwepo\}$.
Let $xkdjshla, \dots, wqtmpdse$ be the elements of $rnszvpqa$ labeled such that
$xmtrawob(xkdjshla) < xmtrawob(sbvcltno) < \cdots < xmtrawob(wqtmpdse)$. We claim that there exists
a unique $mczvkdrl \in \{1, \dots, drxqplou\}$ such that $(plnxryud, tnjgwepo, qzxwvtnp_{mczvkdrl+1})
\in lqmgidest$, where hereafter $qzxwvtnp_{drxqplou+fhzqvemi} = qzxwvtnp_{fhzqvemi}$.

We show existence first. Suppose no such $mczvkdrl$ exists; then for all
$mczvkdrl,fhzqvemi \in \{1, \dots, drxqplou\}$, we have $(qzxwvtnp_{mczvkdrl+fhzqvemi}, tnjgwepo, plnxryud) \notin lqmgidest$.
This holds by property 1 for $fhzqvemi=1$ and by induction on $fhzqvemi$ in
general, noting that
\begin{align*}
(qzxwvtnp_{mczvkdrl+fhzqvemi+1}, tnjgwepo, qzxwvtnp_{mczvkdrl+fhzqvemi}), &(qzxwvtnp_{mczvkdrl+fhzqvemi}, tnjgwepo, plnxryud)  \in lqmgidest \\
&\Rightarrow (qzxwvtnp_{mczvkdrl+fhzqvemi}, qzxwvtnp_{mczvkdrl+fhzqvemi+1}, tnjgwepo), (tnjgwepo, plnxryud, qzxwvtnp_{mczvkdrl+fhzqvemi}) \in lqmgidest \\
&\Rightarrow (qzxwvtnp_{mczvkdrl+fhzqvemi+1},tnjgwepo,plnxryud) \in lqmgidest.
\end{align*}
Applying this when $fhzqvemi=drxqplou$, we get $(qzxwvtnp_{mczvkdrl-1}, tnjgwepo, plnxryud) \in lqmgidest$,
contradicting the fact that $(plnxryud, tnjgwepo, qzxwvtnp_{mczvkdrl-1}) \in lqmgidest$. Hence
existence follows.

Now we show uniqueness. Suppose $(plnxryud, tnjgwepo, qzxwvtnp_{mczvkdrl+1}) \in lqmgidest$; then for
any $nubksyaf \neq mczvkdrl-1, mczvkdrl, mczvkdrl+1$, we have $(plnxryud, qzxwvtnp_{mczvkdrl+1}, qzxwvtnp_{nubksyaf}), (qzxwvtnp_{nubksyaf},
qzxwvtnp_{nubksyaf+1}, plnxryud) \in lqmgidest$ by the
assumption on $bwcrlnaq$. Therefore
\begin{align*}
(plnxryud, tnjgwepo, qzxwvtnp_{mczvkdrl+1}), (qzxwvtnp_{mczvkdrl+1}, qzxwvtnp_{nubksyaf}, plnxryud) \in lqmgidest
&\Rightarrow (qzxwvtnp_{nubksyaf}, plnxryud, tnjgwepo) \in lqmgidest \\
(plnxryud, tnjgwepo, qzxwvtnp_{nubksyaf}), (qzxwvtnp_{nubksyaf}, qzxwvtnp_{nubksyaf+1}, plnxryud) \in lqmgidest
&\Rightarrow (tnjgwepo, qzxwvtnp_{nubksyaf}, qzxwvtnp_{nubksyaf+1}),
\end{align*}
so $(qzxwvtnp_{nubksyaf}, tnjgwepo, qzxwvtnp_{nubksyaf+1}) \notin lqmgidest$. The case $nubksyaf = mczvkdrl+1$ is ruled out by
\[
(plnxryud, tnjgwepo, qzxwvtnp_{mczvkdrl+1}), (qzxwvtnp_{mczvkdrl+1}, qzxwvtnp_{mczvkdrl+2}, plnxryud) \in lqmgidest \Rightarrow (tnjgwepo,
qzxwvtnp_{mczvkdrl+1}, qzxwvtnp_{mczvkdrl+2}) \in lqmgidest
\]
and the case $nubksyaf=mczvkdrl-1$ is similar.

Finally, we put $xmtrawob(tnjgwepo)$ in $(xmtrawob(wqtmpdse), + \infty)$ if $mczvkdrl = drxqplou$, and
$(xmtrawob(plnxryud), xmtrawob(qzxwvtnp_{mczvkdrl+1}))$ otherwise; an analysis similar to that above
shows that $xmtrawob$ has the desired property.

kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout write n:=|A| \ge 3.


1.  The axioms make S a (complete) cyclic order


A ternary relation T on a set X is called a cyclic order if it fulfils
(C1)  (a,b,c)\in T \Longrightarrow (b,c,a)\in T;
(C2)  (a,b,c)\in T \Longrightarrow (c,b,a)\notin T; and
(C3)  whenever (a,b,c),(a,c,d)\in T with a,b,c,d pairwise distinct, then (a,b,d)\in T.

It is classical (and easy to check) that condition (C3) is equivalent to the
four-term exchange used in the statement.  Items 1-3 therefore already yield
(C1)-(C3).  Item 4 additionally guarantees that for every distinct a,b,c
exactly one of the two possible orientations occurs, i.e. the cyclic order is
total (sometimes called **complete**).  From now on we treat S as a fixed
complete cyclic order on A and freely use the standard facts about cyclic
orders.


2.  Deleting one point produces a linear order


Fix an element z\in A and put B:=A\setminus\{z\} (so |B|=n-1).  Define a
binary relation

    x <_{z} y \;\Longleftrightarrow\; (z,x,y) \in S\qquad(x,y\in B).   (2.1)

Lemma 2.1  The relation <_{z} is a strict linear (total) order on B.

Proof.
Anti-symmetry and transitivity follow exactly as in the original write-up; we
only repeat the argument for totality because this is where the new condition
(4) is used.

* (totality)  Let x\ne y in B.  Applying (4) to the three distinct elements
  z,x,y we know that precisely one of (z,x,y) and (z,y,x) is in S.  Hence
  exactly one of x<_{z}y or y<_{z}x holds.
\blacksquare 

Because <_{z} is strict and total we may list the elements of B as

    b_{1} <_{z} b_{2} <_{z} \dots <_{z} b_{m}\qquad(m:=n-1).   (2.2)


3.  Chains in the linear order are compatible with S


Lemma 3.1  If x<_{z}y<_{z}w in B, then (x,y,w)\in S.

Proof.
Exactly as before: (z,x,y),(z,y,w)\in S yields (y,w,z)\in S by cyclicity, and
the four-term rule for z,x,y,w gives (x,y,w)\in S. \blacksquare 

Corollary 3.2  For any indices 1\le i<j<k\le m we have (b_{i},b_{j},b_{k})\in S.
(The corollary follows by repeatedly applying Lemma 3.1.)


4.  A convenient linear embedding of A into \mathbb R


Define g:A\to\mathbb R by

    g(b_{i}) := i \quad(1\le i\le m), \qquad g(z):=m+1.   (4.1)

The map g is clearly injective.  We verify that it has the required property.

Let a,b,c\in A be distinct with g(a)<g(b)<g(c).  Three situations can occur.

Case 1.  None of a,b,c equals z.  Then a=b_{i}, b=b_{j}, c=b_{k} with i<j<k,
and Corollary 3.2 gives (a,b,c)\in S.

Case 2.  c=z.  Then a,b\in B and g(a)<g(b) implies a<_{z}b, i.e. (z,a,b)\in S
by (2.1); cyclicity yields (a,b,z)=(a,b,c)\in S.

Case 3.  The element z would have to be the smallest or middle value, but
by construction g(z)=m+1 is the largest value among all g-images, so this case
cannot occur.

Hence in every possible configuration

g(a)<g(b)<g(c)\;\Rightarrow\;(a,b,c)\in S.

This finishes the proof. \blacksquare

core_steps: Regard properties (1) & (2) as defining a cyclic orientation on every triple.
Do induction on n = |A| (base n = 3 handled by choosing either increasing or decreasing order).
Inductive lemma: for the new element z there is a UNIQUE adjacent pair (a_i , a_{i+1}) in the current linear order with (a_i , z , a_{i+1}) ∈ S (proved by contradiction using the three axioms).
Insert z by picking g(z) strictly between g(a_i) and g(a_{i+1}) (or to the right of all of them when i = n).
Check that this extended g keeps ↔ between ‘cyclicly increasing’ and membership in S; induction completes.

slot1.description: The target set only needs to be a totally ordered set large enough to host |A| distinct values; any such set works.

slot2.description: The direction of the inequality criterion can be reversed (use g' = −g, or decree that decreasing triples lie in S).

slot3.description: The specific choice in the 3-element base case (increasing vs. decreasing assignment) is arbitrary; either orientation starts the induction.
slot3.original: For |A| = 3 pick g(a)<g(b)<g(c) when (a,b,c)∈S, otherwise g(a)>g(b)>g(c)

slot4.description: Any consistent labelling/ordering of B = A\{z} (not necessarily a_1,…,a_n) suffices; only the relative order matters.

slot5.description: The modular indexing convention (a_{n+k}=a_k) is inessential; one could work with indices mod n in any equivalent notation.
